column1: #separator:tab
#html:true
#deck:AI Digital Characters::00_Exam_MC_Traps
Front
column2: Back

column1: The Mel scale is a linear scale that maps frequency to pitch in speech recognition.
column2:
FALSE
Mel is a logarithmic/perceptual scale: Mel = 1127·ln(1 + f/700). It approximates human pitch perception.

Source: exam2024:Q1a, L04:mel

column1: The RNN-Transducer model is designed for offline decoding and does not support streaming speech recognition.
column2:
FALSE
RNN-T supports streaming/online decoding via the prediction network and joint network with blank tokens.

Source: exam2024:Q1b, L04:architectures

column1: According to the James-Lange theory, the perception of an emotion-arousing stimulus leads simultaneously to the experience of emotion and physiological (bodily) changes.
column2:
FALSE
James-Lange: Stimulus → Physiological response → Emotion (sequential). Simultaneous emotion + body is Cannon-Bard.

Source: exam2024:Q1c, solution_ex1:2a

column1: Using convex optimization, a skin conductance signal can be decomposed into phasic and tonic components.
column2:
TRUE
SC decomposition into fast phasic (event-related) and slow tonic (baseline) components uses convex optimization (lecture L02).

Source: exam2024:Q1d, L02:skin_conductance

column1: In the transformer model, positional encoding is used to provide the model with information about the order of tokens in the input sequence.
column2:
TRUE
Self-attention is permutation-invariant; positional encodings inject token position information.

Source: exam2024:Q1e, solution_ex1:3c

column1: Recurrent neural networks (RNNs) can be used for tasks where the input is a sequence of tokens, such as language modeling and sequence classification.
column2:
TRUE
RNNs maintain hidden state across time steps — used for LM, sentiment classification, etc.

Source: exam2024:Q1f, L03:rnn

column1: Pruning involves removing the most impactful connections from a neural network.
column2:
FALSE
Pruning removes least important weights/neurons to compress the model while preserving performance.

Source: exam2024:Q1g, solution_ex2:2b

column1: Quantization reduces both the model size and the model accuracy.
column2:
TRUE
Quantization maps weights/activations to lower precision → smaller model, but typically some accuracy loss (though techniques like SmoothQuant mitigate this).

Source: exam2024:Q1h, L05:quantization

column1: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning operates only on a true subset of the model.
column2:
TRUE
PEFT (LoRA, adapters) freezes most weights and trains only a small subset of additional parameters.

Source: exam2024:Q1i, solution_ex2:1c

column1: The Griffin-Lim algorithm reconstructs the phase information iteratively from just the magnitude spectrogram without using any learned parameters.
column2:
TRUE
Griffin-Lim is an iterative phase-estimation algorithm — no neural network, uses magnitude spectrogram only.

Source: exam2024:Q1j, solution_ex3:3a

column1: A nuclear accent is the last accent in a phrase and is perceived as more prominent.
column2:
TRUE
The nuclear (phrase-final) accent is the most prominent accent in an intonation phrase.

Source: exam2024:Q1k, L08:prosody

column1: In FastSpeech, the speech speeds up when we repeat the encoder states by doubling the predicted duration.
column2:
FALSE
Repeating encoder states lengthens the output → speech slows down. Fewer repeats → faster speech.

Source: exam2024:Q1l, solution_ex3:2c

column1: Autonomous agents are characterized by their ability to operate independently, react to their environment, and plan proactively. They also have control over their internal states.
column2:
TRUE
Classic agent properties: autonomy, reactivity, pro-activeness, social ability (lecture L12).

Source: exam2024:Q1m, L12:agents

column1: Dialogue trees offer high flexibility in responding to unexpected queries, but they are difficult to scale and require manual design for new topics.
column2:
FALSE
Dialogue trees have low flexibility for unexpected queries; they are hard to scale (second part is true).

Source: exam2024:Q1n, solution_ex5:1b

column1: In a reinforcement learning algorithm, the reward should be differentiable so that we can run back-propagation to train the policy based on data.
column2:
FALSE
Rewards are not required to be differentiable. Policy gradients estimate gradients via sampled trajectories.

Source: exam2024:Q1o, solution_ex5:1e

column1: We must collect reinforcement learning trajectories from agent-environment interactions when employing Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) to learn the policy. The collected reinforcement learning trajectories can have various lengths.
column2:
TRUE
PPO is on-policy: collects rollouts from current policy; trajectories can vary in length.

Source: exam2024:Q1p, L14:ppo

column1: The animation principle "Slow In and Slow Out" emphasizes linear motion.
column2:
FALSE
Slow In / Slow Out means ease in and ease out — acceleration/deceleration at start and end, not constant linear speed.

Source: exam2024:Q1q, L10:principles

column1: Facial muscles attach to a bone and connect to the skin. When they contract, they create dynamic wrinkles that form perpendicular to the direction of muscle contraction.
column2:
TRUE
Facial muscles attach bone-to-skin; wrinkles form perpendicular to contraction direction (lecture L10).

Source: exam2024:Q1r, L10:anatomy

column1: Linear blend skinning can handle large joint rotation angles without visual artifacts.
column2:
FALSE
LBS causes candy-wrapper twisting and volume loss at large joint angles due to linear rotation blending.

Source: exam2024:Q1s, solution_ex4:2a5

column1: Artifact detection is essential in affective computing because physiological signals like EEG and ECG can be contaminated by noise from muscle movements or environmental interference.
column2:
TRUE
Biosignals need artifact removal (movement, EMG contamination, etc.) before reliable affect inference.

Source: exam2025:Q1a, L02:artifact_detection

column1: In backpropagation, the gradient of the loss with respect to each weight is computed independently of the activation functions used in the network.
column2:
FALSE
Backprop uses the chain rule — gradients depend on activation function derivatives (ReLU, sigmoid, etc.).

Source: exam2025:Q1b, L03:neural_nets

column1: Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) is commonly applied to short-time frames of a speech signal to analyze how frequency content changes over time.
column2:
TRUE
STFT: window speech → DFT per frame → spectrogram over time.

Source: exam2025:Q1c, L04:feature_extraction

column1: Prosody refers exclusively to the spectral features of speech, such as formants and harmonics, without involving rhythm or intonation.
column2:
FALSE
Prosody includes pitch, duration, loudness, rhythm, intonation — not just spectral features.

Source: exam2025:Q1d, solution_ex3:1c

column1: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning methods (PEFT) like LoRA and adapters reduce computational cost by freezing the majority of a pre-trained model's weights and learning only a small number of additional parameters.
column2:
TRUE
PEFT keeps most LLM weights frozen; trains low-rank adapters or small added layers.

Source: exam2025:Q1e, solution_ex2:1c

column1: Prompt engineering can be used to implicitly control the chatbot's tone, style, and persona without modifying the underlying model.
column2:
TRUE
System prompts, role prompting, and style instructions shape output without weight updates.

Source: exam2025:Q1f, L06:prompting

column1: Hybrid fusion strategies eliminate the need for modality-specific embedding by treating all modalities identically throughout the model pipeline.
column2:
FALSE
Hybrid fusion combines early and late fusion — still uses modality-specific encoders/embeddings.

Source: exam2025:Q1g, L07:multimodality

column1: Stress depends on sentence context, while accent is fixed within the word.
column2:
TRUE
Word accent is lexical (fixed); sentence stress/placement varies with context and emphasis.

Source: exam2025:Q1h, L08:prosody

column1: WaveNet uses causal, dilated convolutions to model long-range temporal dependencies in sequential data such as speech.
column2:
TRUE
WaveNet: stacked dilated causal convolutions for autoregressive waveform generation.

Source: exam2025:Q1i, solution_ex3:3a

column1: In Linear Blend Skinning, each vertex is influenced by exactly one bone.
column2:
FALSE
LBS blends influences from multiple bones with weights w_ij summing to 1.

Source: exam2025:Q1j, solution_ex4:1c

column1: Linear interpolation between animation key frames often results in unnatural or abrupt motion rather than smooth transitions.
column2:
TRUE
Pure linear interpolation lacks ease-in/out → mechanical motion; Disney's Slow In/Out fixes this.

Source: exam2025:Q1k, L10:principles

column1: In normalizing flows, the model learns only the prior distribution pZ, while the transformation functions remain fixed.
column2:
FALSE
Normalizing flows learn invertible transformation functions; change of variables adjusts density via Jacobian determinant.

Source: exam2025:Q1l, solution_ex4:1h

column1: An autonomous agent's actions are determined solely by its immediate perception of the environment.
column2:
FALSE
Agents also use internal state, goals, and proactive planning — not perception alone.

Source: exam2025:Q1m, solution_ex5:1a

column1: A primary disadvantage of dialogue trees is their limited flexibility, which makes it difficult for them to handle unexpected user queries or engage in open-ended conversations.
column2:
TRUE
Dialogue trees are rigid, manually authored, poor at open-domain unexpected input.

Source: exam2025:Q1n, solution_ex5:1b

column1: In on-policy reinforcement learning, data collected from older versions of the policy can be stored in a replay buffer and reused to improve training efficiency.
column2:
FALSE
On-policy methods (PPO) use current policy data only. Replay buffers are off-policy (DQN, SAC).

Source: exam2025:Q1o, L14:rl_algorithms

column1: In the Q-learning algorithm, the policy is implicitly derived by selecting the action with the highest estimated Q-value for a given state, rather than being represented by a separate, explicit policy network.
column2:
TRUE
Q-learning: greedy policy π(s) = argmax_a Q(s,a); no separate policy network needed.

Source: exam2025:Q1p, L14:value_based

column1: Facial expression analysis and blood pressure measurement are both invasive sensing methods.
column2:
FALSE
Facial analysis is non-invasive (video). Blood pressure measurement is invasive/interactive (cuff).

Source: solution_ex1:1a

column1: Action units refer to the motion of the whole body.
column2:
FALSE
AUs are facial muscle movements (FACS), not whole-body motion.

Source: solution_ex1:1b

column1: ECG refers to recording electrical activity captured from the skin's surface.
column2:
TRUE
ECG electrodes on skin record heart's electrical activity.

Source: solution_ex1:1c

column1: Softmax outputs are limited to the range [−1, 1].
column2:
FALSE
Softmax outputs are in [0, 1] and sum to 1 (probabilities).

Source: solution_ex1:1d

column1: RNNs process all time steps simultaneously like Transformers.
column2:
FALSE
RNNs process sequentially; Transformers use parallel self-attention.

Source: solution_ex1:1e

column1: The attention mechanism assigns different weights to different parts of the input.
column2:
TRUE
Attention computes a weighted sum — higher weight = more focus.

Source: solution_ex1:1f

column1: In ADC, quantization converts real-valued numbers to positive numbers only.
column2:
FALSE
Quantization maps to a finite discrete set — can include negative values.

Source: solution_ex1:1g

column1: The fundamental frequency is the highest frequency component of a complex waveform.
column2:
FALSE
Fundamental frequency is the lowest (most dominant periodic) frequency.

Source: solution_ex1:1h

column1: A Mel filter bank consists of overlapping rectangular filters.
column2:
FALSE
Mel filters are overlapping triangular filters spaced on the Mel scale.

Source: solution_ex1:1i

column1: Top-p sampling randomly picks from the p most likely words.
column2:
FALSE
Top-p (nucleus): smallest set whose cumulative prob ≥ p. Top-k picks k most likely.

Source: solution_ex2:1a

column1: More detailed prompts always lead to better AI-generated responses.
column2:
FALSE
Overly detailed/restrictive prompts can reduce creativity and flexibility.

Source: solution_ex2:1b

column1: PEFT reduces the total number of model parameters.
column2:
FALSE
PEFT trains a subset of params; total param count may increase (adapters) but compute cost drops.

Source: solution_ex2:1c

column1: In-context learning involves training with prompt-response pairs.
column2:
FALSE
ICL uses examples at inference without weight updates.

Source: solution_ex2:1d

column1: RAG supplements LLM responses with retrieved external information.
column2:
TRUE
RAG retrieves domain-specific/up-to-date docs to augment generation.

Source: solution_ex2:1e

column1: MOS and A/B testing are identical qualitative evaluation methods.
column2:
FALSE
MOS: absolute rating scale. A/B: pairwise preference comparison.

Source: solution_ex2:1f

column1: Multimodal chatbots can use user state modeling for more empathetic responses.
column2:
TRUE
Voice tone, facial expression, sentiment → adaptive empathetic responses.

Source: solution_ex2:1g

column1: Late fusion is always superior to early fusion.
column2:
FALSE
Choice depends on task; early fusion can capture cross-modal interactions better.

Source: solution_ex2:1h

column1: Chatbot personality uses the same five factors as the human Big Five model.
column2:
FALSE
Chatbot traits: artificiality, conscientiousness, vibrancy, civility, neuroticism.

Source: solution_ex2:1i

column1: Text normalization is only needed to expand abbreviations like Dr. and Mr.
column2:
FALSE
Also: number expansion, punctuation disambiguation, etc.

Source: solution_ex3:1a

column1: ML models can be trained to detect sentence boundaries from punctuation context.
column2:
TRUE
Period can be abbreviation vs. sentence end — ML can disambiguate.

Source: solution_ex3:1b

column1: Prosodic analysis includes loudness but not pitch.
column2:
FALSE
Prosody includes pitch (F0), duration, and loudness.

Source: solution_ex3:1c

column1: Prominence in speech refers to the volume of words only.
column2:
FALSE
Prominence involves pitch, duration, and sometimes loudness — emphasis.

Source: solution_ex3:1d

column1: Putting two prominent accents right next to each other is known as a lapse.
column2:
FALSE
Clash = accents too close. Lapse = accents too far apart.

Source: solution_ex3:1e

column1: Diphone synthesis often lacks natural prosody due to limited prerecorded units.
column2:
TRUE
Small diphone inventory → less natural prosody than unit selection.

Source: solution_ex3:1f

column1: Unit selection synthesis produces more natural speech by choosing larger contextually appropriate segments.
column2:
TRUE
Selects variable-length units from recorded speech for naturalness.

Source: solution_ex3:1g

column1: Concatenative synthesis generates speech by training neural networks on text.
column2:
FALSE
Concatenative/unit selection uses pre-recorded speech segments.

Source: solution_ex3:1h

column1: When SYS adds a word not in REF, that is a deletion error.
column2:
FALSE
Extra word in SYS = insertion. Missing word = deletion.

Source: solution_ex3:1i

column1: MOS uses an absolute scale while A/B tests ask for comparative preference.
column2:
TRUE
MOS: 1–5 quality rating. A/B: prefer A or B.

Source: solution_ex3:1j

column1: Stylized characters are more likely to trigger the uncanny valley than realistic characters.
column2:
FALSE
Realistic characters near-human are more uncanny; stylized avoids the valley.

Source: solution_ex4:1a

column1: In an articulated model, joints are only visual connectors between bones.
column2:
FALSE
Joints define degrees of freedom and how bones rotate/move.

Source: solution_ex4:1b

column1: In LBS, each vertex is influenced by exactly one bone.
column2:
FALSE
Vertices blend multiple bones with weights.

Source: solution_ex4:1c

column1: The Jacobian method is widely used for IK when analytical solutions aren't possible.
column2:
TRUE
Numerical IK via Jacobian pseudoinverse is standard.

Source: solution_ex4:1d

column1: Damping forces depend on spring stiffness.
column2:
FALSE
Damping depends on velocity; stiffness affects spring force.

Source: solution_ex4:1e

column1: Diverse movements are particularly important for stylized characters.
column2:
TRUE
Stylized animation emphasizes expressive, varied motion.

Source: solution_ex4:1f

column1: When m < d, manifold learning performs dimensionality reduction.
column2:
TRUE
High-dimensional data (d) projected to lower intrinsic dimension (m).

Source: solution_ex4:1g

column1: Normalizing flows use invertible functions; the Jacobian determinant adjusts density.
column2:
TRUE
Change of variables: p_X(x) = p_Z(f⁻¹(x)) · |det J_{f⁻¹}(x)|.

Source: solution_ex4:1h

column1: Proactive agents primarily respond to immediate environmental changes.
column2:
FALSE
Reactivity = immediate response. Proactiveness = planning for future opportunities.

Source: solution_ex5:1a

column1: Dialogue trees are highly scalable for new topics.
column2:
FALSE
New topics require manual design — poor scalability.

Source: solution_ex5:1b

column1: Link prediction determines whether a relationship should exist between two entities.
column2:
TRUE
Link prediction infers missing edges in a knowledge graph.

Source: solution_ex5:1c

column1: Knowledge graph embeddings can answer queries on large incomplete graphs.
column2:
TRUE
KGE enables similarity search and link prediction when SPARQL is insufficient.

Source: solution_ex5:1d

column1: In RL, the transition model must be differentiable.
column2:
FALSE
Transition model need not be differentiable; RL handles non-differentiable simulators.

Source: solution_ex5:1e

column1: Monte Carlo policy gradient requires on-policy trajectories.
column2:
TRUE
Policy gradient methods collect trajectories from the current policy.

Source: solution_ex5:1f

column1: The optimal policy maximizes each individual future reward step.
column2:
FALSE
Policy maximizes expected discounted return, not each individual reward.

Source: solution_ex5:1g

column1: Actor-Critic methods use a value function as baseline to reduce variance.
column2:
TRUE
Critic estimates V(s) or Q(s,a) to reduce variance of policy gradient estimates.

Source: solution_ex5:1h
